coredata.dc:title: One-component dual-mode sensor array for identification and quantification of biothiols
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ning, Yuanna
Wang, Yongbo
Wang, Shaojie
Wang, Wencai
coredata.dc:description: Biothiol analysis is significant to health assessment and early detection of potential diseases. Considering practical requirements, simple and rapid identification and determination of biothiols are still a great challenge due to the similar structures. Fortunately, the recently emerging colorimetric sensor array technique makes such a task possible. Herein, one-component dual-mode sensor array consisting of carbon dots (CDs) and Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) system was designed for identification and quantification of biothiols. The identification principle is based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and different binding constants. Due to the IFE between CDs and AgNPs, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched, but recovered again after addition of biothiols because of the binding of biothiols with AgNPs. Significantly, the fluorescence recovered in varying degree due to the different binding constants of biothiols to AgNPs. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the system decreased and the color of the solution deepened. Therefore, the CDs-AgNPs system with fluorescence and absorbance response was used as the single sensing unit and generated the cross-responsive signal for different biothiols. The sensor array achieved 100 % accuracy in identifying biothiols and biothiol mixtures. Moreover, the rapid quantification of biothiols in serum samples was also achieved by RGB-based smartphone colorimetry. The way to construct one component sensor array with dual mode signal outputs tremendously saves cost and time, providing a powerful tool for the identification of different biothiols. In addition, the rapid quantification of biothiols in serum samples based on RGB-based smartphone colorimetry demonstrated its powerful application prospects in disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biothiols
Sensor array
Carbon dots
Ag nanoparticles
Dual mode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The dual-mode response of CDs-AgNPs system to biothiols
Construction of one-component dual-mode sensor array
The dual-mode sensor array for identification of biothiols
Application of the sensor array in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, AgNPs, and CDs-AgNPs; insets are photographs of CDs, AgNPs and CDs-AgNPs solutions in daylight. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of AgNPs and excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of CDs; insets are photographs of AgNPs and CDs in daylight and fluorescence images of CDs in 365 nm UV light, respectively. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths and (d) 3D fluorescence spectra.

(a) The dual-mode response of the sensor array to different biothiols. (b) Heat map of the dual-mode response. (c) Canonical score plot for the first two factors of response patterns obtained from the sensor array with different biothiols.
LDA plots for binary mixtures of biothiols with different molar ratios: (a) Cys + Hcy, (b) Cys + GSH, and (c) Hcy + GSH. (d) LDA plots for ternary mixtures with different molar ratios.

Classical score plots of (a) Cys, (b) Hcy, and (c) GSH at different concentrations. The corresponding radar plots of (a1) Cys, (b1) Hcy, and (c1) GSH at different concentrations. The linear relationship between factor 1 and the concentrations of (a2) Cys, (b2) Hcy, and (c2) GSH.

Smartphone-assisted fluorescence and colorimetric quantification of biothiols. Fluorescence images under UV lamp (365 nm) captured by a smartphone, RGB values analyzed by color recognition software, and the calibration curves of (a) Cys, (b) Hcy, and (c) GSH. The images under daylight, RGB values, and the calibration curves of (a1) Cys, (b1) Hcy, and (c1) GSH.

Schematic illustration of the one-component dual-mode sensor array based on CDs-AgNPs system. (a) The design of the sensor array based on CDs and AgNPs. (b) The identification of biothiols by LDA method and quantification of biothiols based on change of fluorescence and solution color by smartphone.
Determination of biothiols in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: One-component dual-mode sensor array for identification and quantification of biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological and Pharmaceutical Sciences
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Xinghuacun College, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science, Xinghuacun College, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the one-component dual-mode sensor array based on CDs-AgNPs system.

Biothiol analysis is significant to health assessment and early detection of potential diseases. Considering practical requirements, simple and rapid identification and determination of biothiols are still a great challenge due to the similar structures. Fortunately, the recently emerging colorimetric sensor array technique makes such a task possible. Herein, one-component dual-mode sensor array consisting of carbon dots (CDs) and Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) system was designed for identification and quantification of biothiols. The identification principle is based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and different binding constants. Due to the IFE between CDs and AgNPs, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched, but recovered again after addition of biothiols because of the binding of biothiols with AgNPs. Significantly, the fluorescence recovered in varying degree due to the different binding constants of biothiols to AgNPs. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the system decreased and the color of the solution deepened. Therefore, the CDs-AgNPs system with fluorescence and absorbance response was used as the single sensing unit and generated the cross-responsive signal for different biothiols. The sensor array achieved 100 % accuracy in identifying biothiols and biothiol mixtures. Moreover, the rapid quantification of biothiols in serum samples was also achieved by RGB-based smartphone colorimetry. The way to construct one component sensor array with dual mode signal outputs tremendously saves cost and time, providing a powerful tool for the identification of different biothiols. In addition, the rapid quantification of biothiols in serum samples based on RGB-based smartphone colorimetry demonstrated its powerful application prospects in disease diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols exist in the human body mainly in the form of low molecular weight thiols or protein thiols, including mainly cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH) [1], which are crucial for physiological functions such as redox dynamic balance and metabolic maintenance [2]. Abnormal intracellular thiol levels can cause many potential diseases. For instance, aberrant Cys levels cause growth retardation, liver damage, edema, lethargy, weakness, and muscle deterioration in children [3–5]. Excessive Hcy levels in the body cause Hyperhomocysteinaemia, which increases the risk of cerebral thrombosis, dementia, coronary heart disease, high blood pressure, and other diseases [6,7]. GSH imbalance causes Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, and cancers, etc [8,9]. Therefore, the construction of a straightforward and sensitive method for the analysis of biothiols is essential for human physiological health and the early diagnosis of certain diseases, so that early measures can be taken to intervene in the prevention and treatment of the disease.

So far, several methods have been developed by previous authors to analyze biothiols, such as mass spectrometry [10], high-performance liquid chromatography [11], electrochemical method [12], chemiluminescence [13], fluorescence method, colorimetric method, and other techniques [14]. However, these strategies are laborious, costly, complicated to operate, and have only a single signal pattern, which can only identify specific biothiols but cannot distinguish multiple biothiols at the same time. Because of the very analogous molecule structure and chemical properties of biothiols, the detection and differentiation of biological thiols remains an enormous obstacle. Fortunately, the sensor array strategy provides an effective solution for the detection and identification of various thiols and their mixtures. Inspired by the systems of mammalian olfaction and gustation, array-based sensors consist of a sequence of interrelated, non-selective receptors, which combine with multiple kinds of target analytes to generate a distinct “fingerprint” response [15–17]. Unlike “key-lock” sensors, which are composed of specific receptors, sensor arrays do not require highly selective sensing units. This increases the labor and time efficiency of implementing systems of detection that depend on sensor arrays.

In recent years, a variety of nanomaterials have become excellent array sensing elements. Among these, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have drawn considerable interest because of their low cytotoxicity, superior optical qualities, significant biocompatibility, and ease of manufacture [18]. Various quantum dots (QDs)-based sensor arrays have been designed to recognize and detect biothiols. For instance, Zhang’s [19] team combined three blue fluorescent CDs made of amino acids with Ag+ to form a CDs-Ag+ composite system, which permits the creation of an innovative sensor array-based method for biothiol detection and discrimination. Zhou’s et al. [20] created nanoenzymes called metal ion-doped carbon dots (M-CDs) and used their peroxidase-like activity to catalyze the colorimetric reaction of the substrates. This allowed detection of a wide variety of biothiols, demonstrating their potential use in disease diagnosis. Using a multiple-channel sensor array based on trichromatic luminescent CDs-metal ion pairs (Cu2+, Ag+, and Fe3+), Gao et al. [21] were able to detect biological thiols with great potential for clinical diagnosis. The array effectively discriminated each thiol within serum and urine mediums and was able to identify the cell types of normal or cancerous cells. Colorimetric and fluorescence sensor arrays are widely used [22–24], but they usually have only one response mode, and a single colorimetric method produces a large error, and the fluorescence method is easily disturbed by the background fluorescence [25,26]. Therefore, combining colorimetric and fluorescence methods to construct a bimodal sensor array is an effective way to avoid errors and amplify signals. Highly significant for the realization of high-throughput, high-sensitivity, and low-cost in situ detection is the development of colorimetric and fluorescent bimodal probes based on nanomaterials [27–29].

Here, as illustrated in Scheme 1 , we present a colorimetric and fluorescent double-modal nanosensor array made of CDs and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for the detection and differentiation of biothiols. A one-step hydrothermal method using urea and citric acid as sources of carbon and nitrogen was used to produce the CDs. The fluorescence of CDs quenched significantly due to the inner filter effect (IFE) because of the spectrum overlap between AgNPs absorption and CD emission. When the biothiols are present, the biothiols have a greater ability to bind with AgNPs, causing the separation of CDs from the AgNPs surface and thus the recovery of the CDs fluorescence. Due to the different binding constants of the biothiols to AgNPs, the intensity of fluorescence was restored to different degrees, and the solution color also deepened. The variations in the intensity of fluorescence and the absorbance are used as the output signals to form the fingerprints of the characteristics of each biothiols. The response patterns were then analyzed using linear discriminant analysis (LDA), the identification and quantification of single biological and the mixed biothiols were ahcived. In addition, the sensor array showed good performance in detection of multiple biothiols in real biological samples, which suggesting a potential application for point-of-care testing (POCT).

We purchased fetal bovine serum (M6546), homocysteine (Hcy), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), histidine (His), aspartate (Asp), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Trp), valine（Val）, threonine（Thr）, tyrosine（Tyr）, glutamate（Glu）, proline（Pro）, ascorbic acid（AA）, folic acid（FA）, and mineral salt (Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Zn2+) through Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Macklin Biochemical Technology Company supplied the following chemicals: potassium bromide (KBr), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), urea (CH4N2O), trisodium (C6H5Na3O7), 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), and citric acid monohydrate (C6H10O8). Prepare the 0.01 M Hepes buffer by mixing 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazine ethanesulfonic acid (1.19 g) with 500 mL deionized water, adjusting pH to 7. Without any additional purification, the reagents in the experiment are all analytical grade. Throughout the course of the study, ultra-purified water (18.2 MΩ) was utilized.

The FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) fitted with a 2.0 nm slit was used to measure the fluorescence spectra. Multifunctional enzyme labeler (Thermo, Germany) and the Cary 500 UV spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) were used to obtain absorbance and UV–vis absorption spectra, respectively. Images from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were captured using a high-resolution TEM, a JEOL JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) instrument, and analyzed for particle size using Nano Measurer software. A Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK) was used to measure zeta potentials. The data about the functional structures and the constituent elements of the CDs was acquired by means of vacuum FT-IR spectroscopy using a Bruker VERTEX 70v (Bruker, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) using Escalab 250Xi (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were determined using FL-1000 (Edinburgh, UK). Fluorescence images taken with a smartphone under 365 nm laser light.

The optical properties, including fluorescence and absorbance, were analyzed by in situ detection in the liquid state. For the TEM characterization, the CDs-AgNPs solution was configured and sonicated to make it uniformly dispersed, and then the sample was aspirated and added dropwise to a copper grid, finally, the sample was dried and examined by TEM. For the characterization of FT-IR and XPS, the solution of CDs-AgNPs mixed system were freeze-dried to obtain a powder sample for testing.

The hydrothermal process was used to generate CDs, with urea and citric acid providing the carbon and nitrogen resources [30]. In summary, 40 mL of deionized water was used to dissolve 0.2 g each of citric acid and urea. Once the mixture was sufficiently dissolved, 100 μL of concentrated sulfuric acid was added. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. Once it has cooled to ambient temperature, filter with filter paper and centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 10 min. After that, the mixture was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed (500 Da) for a duration of 12 h in order to eliminate surplus precursors and tiny molecules. The prepared CDs solution was freeze dried to remove the solvent and obtain CDs powder. The 0.28 g of CD powder was redissolved in 300 mL deionized water to obtain the CDs solution with a concentration of 0.93 mg/mL. For use in subsequent experiments, the CDs solution was collected and stored at 4 °C. AgNPs were made through the literature as a guide [31,32]. Briefly, the prepared 17.0 mM trisodium citrate 4.0 mL and 18.0 mM AgNO3 4.0 mL solutions were first added to 80 mL deionized water and placed on a magnetic stirrer with vigorous stirring. After 10 min of stirring, 40.0 mL of the newly made NaBH4 (3.0 mM) solution was gradually added by drops to the process. The reaction was continued for 30 min at a constant temperature, and a yellow solution of AgNPs was formed. Subsequently, a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane was used to filter the AgNPs, which were then refrigerated at 4.0 ± 2.0 °C for future use.

The resulting CDs solution was diluted 10 times, and 100 μL CDs were taken and combined with various amounts of AgNPs solution (0–240 μL) to quench the fluorescence of the CDs solution, from which the corresponding spectral data were collected. The CDs-AgNPs system (CDs:AgNPs = 1:2) was used as the sensor element. In addition, the selectivity and specificity of the CDs-AgNPs system were investigated by analyzing the fluorescence response of the system to the potential interferents. The interferents include amino acids (Val, Thr, Tyr, Glu, and Pro, Ala, Arg, Gly, Ser, His, Asp, Lys, Met, Phe, Leu, Trp), vitamins (AA and FA), and ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Zn2+). In addition, the stability of the CDs-AgNPs system was also investigated by recording the fluorescence intensity and the absorbance of the CDs-AgNPs system at different time (1–48 h) and different temperature (5–60 °C).

In this work, using the CDs-AgNPs system with dual-mode signal (fluorescence and absorption) as a cross-responsive sensing unit, a one-component dual-mode sensor array was designed and constructed. Varying concentrations of 100 μL Cys/Hcy/GSH mixed with 300 μL CDs-AgNPs solution and 0.01 M Hepes buffe were added to make the entire volume 1 mL. Following homogenization of the mixture and a 20-minute room-temperature incubation period, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm and absorbance at 400 nm were measured. In order to identify different biothiols, the corresponding F/F0 and A/A0 were calculated and utilized as cross-reacting responses. (F0 and F indicate the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample and the addition of biothiols, and A0 and A indicate the absorbance of the blank sample and the addition of biothiols.) Parameters such as pH, reaction time and CD volume were investigated for optimum performance. For getting a training data matrix consisting of three biothiols × two arrays × six replicates, the test was performed six times for each biothiol. Analyzing the acquired data was done using linear discriminant analysis (LDA), which was conducted using Origin Pro 2021 software (OriginLab, USA). For quantitative calculations of biothiols, the photos of the samples under UV lamp (365 nm) and daylight were obtained by a smartphone and then transferred to RGB data via the color recognition software on the smartphone.

Typically, fetal bovine serum and artificial urine were chosen to simulate real samples. After centrifuging the serum for 20 min at 4000 rpm, 1 mL from the supernatant was collected and diluted 100 times. The urine was diluted 50 times with 0.01 M Hepes buffer and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove insoluble material. The identification of each biothiol and the biothiol mixtures with different ratios in serum and urine and the quantification of biothiols by RGB-based smartphone colorimetry were conducted. The steps and the instrument setup parameters were the same as the above experimental procedures. Ellman’s reagent method is mainly used to detect sulfhydryl compounds [33]. First, 0.4 g of DTNB was dissolved in 100 mL of PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0) to obtain the DTNB reagent. Then, 0.1 mL of DTNB reagent was added to treated fetal bovine serum and artificial urine to which different concentrations of biothiols were added to a total volume of 1.0 mL. Absorbance at 412 nm was measured after incubation for 15 min at room temperature.

The morphology and dimensions of CDs, AgNPs, and CDs-AgNPs composites were investigated through TEM. The TEM photographs presented in Fig. S1a demonstrate that CDs have a uniformly spherical morphology, a size distribution ranging from 2.6 to 5.4 nm, and a mean particle size of 3.84 ± 0.48 nm. The crystal structure of the CDs is evident in high-resolution TEM images, with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the lattice spacing of planar graphene (inset of Fig. S1a). Silver nitrate was reduced by citrate to obtain AgNPs. The TEM images in Fig. S1b show that the synthesized AgNPs have geometrical shapes such as polyhedral and spherical, and the average particle size of AgNPs is 20.74 ± 5.82 nm. The TEM images of the CDs-AgNPs composite system show that gathering of AgNPs occurs after the addition of the CDs solution, and the average particle size is 22.39 ± 6.37 nm (Fig. S1c).

In addition, FT-IR spectroscopy was utilized to discover information on the functional groups of CDs. The broad absorption peaks located at 3306 cm−1 and 3039 cm−1 stem from O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, as illustrated in Fig. S2a. The S–H stretching vibration is correlated with the absorption peak at 2481 cm−1. The C–C, C–N, and C–O stretching vibrations appear at 1427 cm−1, 1374 cm−1, and 1094 cm−1, respectively, and the C = O and C = C stretching vibrations are attributed to two peaks at 1674 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1, respectively. Taken together, these results indicate that the prepared CDs contain a significant number of hydroxyl and amidogen, which ensures that the CDs have good hydrophilicity. The –NH2 and –COOH group on the surface of CDs can form coordination bonds with the silver atoms on the surface of AgNPs, which caused the self-assembly of CDs on the surface of AgNPs. After mixing with AgNPs, the O–H peaks of the FTIR spectra of CDs-AgNPs were significantly wider, indicating that complexation between CDs and AgNPs occurs [34]. The elemental composition and functional groups of CDs as well as the CDs-AgNPs composite system were further analyzed by XPS. The results of the XPS full-scan spectra indicated that CDs consisted of the elements C, N, O, and S (Fig. S2b). The peaks appearing at 368 eV after the composite of CDs with AgNPs represented Ag 3d. According to the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. S3a), the three different peaks at 284.8, 285.7, and 288.5 eV illustrate that C is mostly found as C–C/C = C, C–N/C–O, and C = O. As shown in Fig. S3b, the N 1s spectrum has two peaks at 399.8 eV and 401.5 eV attributed to N–H and C–N. There are two peaks in the high-resolution O 1s spectra (Fig. S3c) at 531.5 eV and 532.6 eV, which correspond for C = O and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively. The peaks at 168.0 and 169.0 eV in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. S3d) correspond to the binding energy of the C–S–H moiety. FT-IR and XPS results showed that the prepared CDs were co-doped with elements N and S and contained many hydrophilic groups, including carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, and sulfhydryl, which facilitated the assembly of the CDs on the surface of the metal nanoparticles. To assess the stability of the system, the Zeta potentials of CDs, AgNPs, CDs-AgNPs, and CDs-AgNPs/(Cys, Hcy, GSH) were investigated, and it is evident from Fig. S2c that the CDs and AgNPs were negatively charged, and the negative charge of CDs is much smaller than that of AgNPs, indicating that the electrostatic repulsion was weak.

The optical characteristics of CDs and AgNPs were investigated using spectrograms. As illustrated by Fig. 1 a, the CDs have a characteristic absorption peak at 330 nm, which is primarily attributable to the n-π* jump of the C = O bond [35]. AgNPs has a broader UV absorption peak at 400 nm, which is not shifted by the addition of CDs solution. AgNPs have no fluorescence, even after absorbing the emission from the CDs (Fig. S4). Under daylight, the CDs solution was colorless and transparent, the AgNPs solution was yellow colloidal, and the mixture of CDs-AgNPs solution was light yellow (inset of Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b demonstrates that the produced CDs had optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 320 nm and 425 nm, respectively. The CD solution displayed a bright blue color when exposed to 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 1b). The fluorescence emission spectra and 3D fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1c and d show that the emitted wavelength of the prepared CDs was red-shifted while the excitation light was adjusted from 290 nm to 390 nm, showing excitation-dependent properties. In general, the excitation-dependent luminescence behavior of CDs can be attributed to the differences in particle dimension and surface state distribution of CDs [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dual-mode response of CDs-AgNPs system to biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: The –NH2 and –COOH groups of CD allow it to be tightly attached to metal nanoparticles, which causes CDs to self-assemble on AgNP surfaces [37–40]. According to the absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1b), the considerable overlap between AgNPs and CDs were observed, leading to the fluorescence quenching of CDs due to the IFE across CDs and AgNPs [41,42]. However, after the addition of biothiols, the CDs were desorbed away from the surface of AgNPs due to the competitive binding of the biothiols with AgNPs. The TEM images of the CDs-AgNPs system showed that the particles became more dispersed after the addition of biothiols, confirming the separation of CDs from AgNPs (Fig. S5). Therefore, recovery of fluorescence was observed for the CDs-AgNPs system, and the restoration of fluorescence correlated well with the biothiol concentration. In other words, the affinity of biothiols for AgNPs is stronger than that of CDs, and the former can preferentially encapsulate AgNPs and block the binding of CDs to AgNPs. The CDs, CDs-AgNPs, and CDs-AgNPs/(Cys, Hcy, GSH) fluorescence spectra were measured. As apparent in Fig. S6c, AgNPs were able to efficiently quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs, which was then restored by the addition of biothiols (Cys, Hcy, and GSH). Significantly, the degree of recovery of the fluorescence was different. This phenomenon suggests that the fluorescence probe based on CDs-AgNPs system can be used for the identification and determination of biothiols.

To more precisely verify the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs-AgNPs were explored as shown in Fig. S2d. The CDs showed a fluorescence lifetime of 5.298 ns, and after adding AgNPs at several different concentrations, the fluorescence lifetimes of 5.330 ns, 5.307 ns, and 5.330 ns were largely unchanged, and the fluorescence decay curves of CDs remained constant. The results displayed that fluorescence quenching was not connected with energy transfer. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of AgNPs is the result of IFE.

The varying degree of the recovery of fluorescence suggested that the different biothiols have different affinity for AgNPs. To further confirm the fact, fluorescence titration experiments were carried out to quantitatively analyze the binding properties and sensitivity of the probes, as demonstrated by Fig. S7. The fluorescence spectra of Cys, Hcy, and GSH were tested with increasing concentrations, respectively. The maximum fluorescence intensity at 425 nm corresponding to various concentrations of biothiols were selected, and the binding constants of the probe to the three biothiols were calculated using the Benesi-Hildebrand formula [43]. The formula was calculated as 1/ΔI = 1/ΔImax + (1/KA[C])(1/ΔImax), Where ΔI = I − I0, ΔImax = Imax − I0, Imax is the fluorescence intensity at saturation of the biothiols concentration, I0 is the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample, I is the fluorescence intensity with the addition of different concentrations of biothiols, and KA and [C] are the binding constants and the concentration of the biothiols, respectively. The binding constants of Cys, Hcy, and GSH were calculated by the Benesi-Hildebrand formula to be 7.7091 × 104 M−1, 7.9190 × 104 M−1, and 4.1911 × 104 M−1, respectively. The results offer a routine for designing a new-type one-component multiple-channel sensor array for the detection of biothiols.

In addition, the stability of the CDs-AgNPs system was also investigated. The fluorescence intensity and the absorbance of the CDs-AgNPs system were recorded at 1 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, 36 h and 48 h, respectively. As shown in the Fig. S8a, the fluorescence intensity and absorbance remain basically unchanged over 48 h, indicating the good photostability. In addition, the influence of temperature on the stability of the detection system was also investigated. As shown in the Fig. S8b, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-AgNPs composite system is stable in the temperature range from 5 °C to 60 °C, while the absorbance was more sensitive to the temperature. For the AgNPs, higher or lower temperature will change their agglomeration state and alter their surface properties, resulting in a decrease in absorbance. However, we can see form the Fig. S11b that the absorbance of the system was relatively stable in the temperature range from 20 °C to 40 °C, which is the temperature range where the detection system is used in general. Therefore, the results indicated that the proposed detection system is stable for the general use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of one-component dual-mode sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimal analytical conditions for determining biothiols, the effects of critical factors on the fluorescence response were examined, including the amount of AgNPs, reaction time, and system pH. The optimal volume ratio of CDs and AgNPs in the composite system is the key to construct the fluorescent probe system and realize the fluorescence sensing of biothiols. As shown in Fig. S6a and b, different volumes of AgNPs colloidal solution were added to the CDs solution to determine the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs and the UV absorbance of AgNPs, and the optimal volume ratio of CDs-AgNPs was determined. As the volume of AgNPs solution (0–240 μL) increased, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was gradually quenched and the UV absorbance of AgNPs was gradually increased. Ultimately, in order to obtain an optimal quenching effect and account for the signal inaccuracy carried on by the high concentration of AgNPs, 200 μL of AgNPs was employed in the subsequent tests. As was observed in Fig. S9a and b, it suggested that when three different types of biothiols (Cys, Hcy, and GSH) were added to the CDs-AgNPs system and allowed to react for 20 min, the fluorescence emission intensity and UV absorbance were stabilized. In addition, the effect of pH on the detection performance was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S9c, all three biothiols led to the recovery of fluorescence signals at pH 4.0, whereas at pH 7.0, merely Cys and Hcy led to the restoration of luminescence signals of CDs-AgNPs. This is because further deprotonation of GSH molecules occurs in acidic medium and GSH molecules have higher negative charge density. In contrast, at pH 7.0, the dissociation of the carboxyl groups and the Steric effect prevent the GSH molecule from penetrating the CDs outer shell layer and interacting with AgNPs. It thus was unable to trigger CDs to detached from AgNPs surface. The Cys and Hcy molecules largely retained their negative charge densities, and they are able to penetrate the CDs shell layer, wrap the AgNPs, and induce the detachment of the CDs to restore the fluorescence. The absorbance values of CDs-AgNPs solutions at 400 nm change when 100 μM biothiols are added to different pH solutions, as shown in Fig. S9d. It is evident that the absorbance increased gradually as the pH of the solution increased and reached the maximum value at pH 7.0. At pH 7.0, the biothiols can be combined with AgNPs through the –SH group to form a composite system with high stability [36].

To ensure the job performance of the sensor array in complex matrices, common amino acids (Val, Thr, Tyr, Glu, and Pro, Ala, Arg, Gly, Ser, His, Asp, Lys, Met, Phe, Leu, Trp), vitamins (AA and FA), and ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Zn2+) were selected as interfering substances for selectivity experiments. As shown in Fig. S10, the CD-AgNPs probes showed weak fluorescence and absorbance responses to the interferants, indicating that the constructed sensor array was highly selective for biothiols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dual-mode sensor array for identification of biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optical response, one component dual-mode sensor array based on CDs-AgNPs system for detection of three major biothiols (Cys, Hcy, and GSH) was designed. The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-AgNPs system in presence of three biothiols are shown in Fig. S6c and d. The different binding constants of Cys, Hcy, and GSH with AgNPs resulting in varying changes in fluorescence and absorbance. The response signals were further visualized using the heatmap (Fig. 2 b) to highlight the differences between the biothiols, and the response data matrix (2 sensors × 3 targets × 6 replicates) was employed as LDA training data to obtain the characteristic fingerprint map and the typical score map (Fig. 2a and c). Based on data processing, the 18 training samples were categorized into 3 independent groups, and the accuracy was 100 %, as shown by the 2D typical score plot (Fig. 2c). In addition, photographs of the CDs-AgNPs systems were taken before and after the reaction of the three biothiols, and notable distinctions were visible to the naked eye (Fig. S11, implying that the visual detection of biothiols can be achieved.

It was considered that the three biothiols can coexist, and thus the capacity of the sensor array to discriminate mixtures was also evaluated. Their pure biothiol, binary biothiol mixtures with different molar ratios, and ternary biothiols mixtures (total concentration of 100 μM) were distinguished using this sensor array. The pure biothiol and biothiols mixtures were observed to exhibit distinct clustering and separation from one another, as depicted in Fig. 3 . The LDA plot indicated that the clusters of each biothiol mixture sample were approximately situated between the corresponding two pure biothiol, indicating a highly ordered distribution characteristic. This result indicates that the sensor array has an excellent discriminatory capacity for the biothiols mixtures.

To further confirm the quantitative analysis capability of the sensor array, three biothiols (concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM, respectively) were quantified separately. As shown in the LDA plot (Fig. 4 (a–c)), the different concentrations of the three biothiols had a continuous spatial distribution with no overlap between clusters. This indicates that over a wide range of concentrations, the sensor array has a good detection capability [44]. The radar plot (Fig. 4(a1–c1)) clearly shows the degree of interaction between the biothiols and the sensing unit, indicating that the change in fluorescence intensity and absorbance raised with increasing analyte concentration. Different concentrations of biothiols are regularly arranged in the upward direction of factor 1. The factor 1 was chosen as the key to quantify different biotiols due to the extremely high percentage of factor 1 (about 99 %). As shown in the Fig. 4(a2–c2), there is a good linear relationship between factor 1 and the concentration of biothiols. The linearity of the concentration–response curves indicated that the interaction of biothiols with CDs-AgNPs was homogeneous and stable, and the sensor arrays had good reproducibility. Thus, the sensor array can accomplish the quantitative detection of biothiols.

In addition, based on the change of fluorescence and the absorption, a visual method for quantitative analysis has been developed. The color changes in fluorescence and daylight modes in the presence of various concentrations of biothiols were obtained using a smartphone as well as color recognition software (Color Grab 3.9.2) to obtain the RGB values, which in turn enabled the visual detection of biothiols. As shown in Fig. 5 , the RGB values showed a good linear relationship with the concentrations of biothiols, and the linear equations for Cys, Hcy, and GSH in fluorescence mode were y = 0.877x + 354.831 (R2 = 0.976), y = 0.991x + 402.943 (R2 = 0.975), and y = 0.611x + 355.591 (R2 = 0.974). In the same way, the linear equations for Cys, Hcy, and GSH in daylight mode were y = 554.450–1.310x (R2 = 0.974), y = 493.868–1.014x (R2 = 0.950), and y = 516.067–1.002x (R2 = 0.967). The limits of detection (LOD) were 1.08 μM, 1.39 μM, and 1.41 μM, respectively. The relationships between the RGB values and biothiol concentrations at low concentrations (5–20 μM) were also investigated. As shown in the Fig. S12, there is also a linear relationship at the low concentration although it is not very good as the relationship at high concentrations.

Additionally, the sensitivity of our method was compared with that of methods in the literature (Table S1). It suggested that our method is not all superior to the reported methods in terms of LOD. However, our method shows many advantages. The designed sensor array is easy to prepare and can be used for identification of different biothiols. Moreover, the colorimetric detection based on smartphone is simple and rapid, which has important application potential for real-time detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the sensor array in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is commonly known that many kinds of diseases are closely correlated with biothiol levels, so it is particularly important to detect biothiols in biological fluids. To assess the performance of the sensor array to detection biothiols in a real environment, fetal bovine serum and artificial urine were selected as the real samples. As shown in Fig. S13 and S14, each biothiol and ternary biothiols mixtures with different molar ratios in serum and urine samples were divided into different clusters on the LDA plot with no overlap between each cluster. Thus, these results demonstrate the significant potential of this sensor array to detect biothiols in real biological samples.

Additionally, the colorimetric quantification of biothiols in serum and urine samples via smartphone was executed. The serum and urine samples spiked with biothiol concentrations of 5, 10, and 20 μM were used to check the quantitative capacity of the sensor array in real samples. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of biothiols in serum samples were in the range of 92.40–106.55 % with the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) in the range of 1.03–6.82 %, and the recoveries of biothiols in the urine samples were in the range of 91.70–108.90 % with RSD in the range of 2.42–6.59 %. These outcomes illustrate that the designed sensor array might be applicable to the analysis of biothiols in biological samples. To verify the accuracy of the designed detection system, Ellman’s method was used for detecting the biothiols in the real samples [33,45,46]. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries were 90.60–106.00 % with the RSD of 1.33–5.52 %, which was consistent with those obtained by our method. The results indicated that our method is capable of quantitatively detecting biothiols in real biological samples, and the results are accurate and reliable.

In summary, we have constructed a one-component dual-mode sensor array based on CDs-AgNPs system for identification and quantitation of biothiols (Cys, Hcy, and GSH). The fluorescence of CDs quenched obviously due to the IFE between CDs and AgNPs and recovered again after the addition of biothiols. Significantly, the degree of recovery was different because the different binding constants of biothiols to AgNPs. In addition, the absorbance decreased and the color of the solution deepened simultaneously. Different biothiols differed in the ability to bind to the AgNPs and produced different fluorescence and colorimetric response signals, which generate unique fingerprint patterns for each biothiols based on the response patterns. Based on the LDA, the dual-mode response patterns can be transformed into two-dimensional clustering profiles and precise identification of different biothiols was achieved. The sensor array was also demonstrated to be capable of recognizing various concentrations of biothiols and mixed biothiol mixtures in different ratios. Additionally, the sensor array was exploited to quantitation of biothiols through the fluorescence and colorimetric response by the smartphone. Notably, identification and quantitation of biothiols in serum samples can also be achieved. Therefore, our proposed sensor array offers a novel method for the straightforward and efficient identification and quantification of biothiols and real-time detection of biothiols in real samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32001783) and the Key R & D Program from Science and Technology Department of Shaanxi Province (2024SF-GJHX-12).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125555.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
